speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4, Sari Baron being interviewed by Betsy Stern, November 5, 1996. So you had two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1952, I had my son, and then I stopped working. And then I started to fear that I need to-- by that time, I belonged to Organization. So I started to work for ORT, because ORT was the one that had given me a chance in Germany, to give me the certificate even though I-- you know, I wasn't-- I just walked into them, because I told them that I had a trade, but I don't have no papers for it. And they tested me and they gave me the certificate that I was a dressmaker. And I felt, when I came to America, as long as they helped me, that I would help them back for the same reason that somebody else should be able to get it for them. And so I started to organize with ORT, and we were doing different thing. And then I belonged to the synagogue and sisterhood. And I-- I really started for nothing, but I become president in the group afterwards. And I kept on going in. In those days, Israel become a state and they needed money, so I worked for the Israeli Bond. Matter of fact, this, you had to buy a lot of bond to able to gain something like that. And whatever-- whatever they needed, I was always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did some work organizing, at the very beginning, some Holocaust survivors to give testimony as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was much later. This was already in the '70s. But from 1963, I become sisterhood president and, also, they were honoring me for ORT. Around the same time, I was queen for a day. And I become United Jewish Council Vice President, and Israeli bond chairman. I don't know, I worked on many, many different organizations. And also, the refugee settlement. When they came from Poland, they didn't know where to go. They'd find my house, and that's where I took in everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I always felt that my parents were helping everybody. And I felt, as I was helped when I came to this country, and this is part of our life, and we have to do that. We have to share things. I always believed in sharing things.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're really coming to a time when you can tell us, what-- for people listening to your story-- what would you like them to hear from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want-- I want to only tell them that, thank God, they're living in the best country, in America. And if you really want to work hard, you could make everything. Because we came to this country without a penny. And we got here in 1949. In 1956, we were able to buy our own house. We didn't ask anybody for anything. We bought new furniture. We bought a new car. I had two little children. And just basically from hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there should be no complain. And if anybody is complaining, I would like to take a train trip with them to Europe and show them what people have there, and what we have here, and they-- just to know how to appreciate their own country. Thank you very, very much for telling us you story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if that's a good message or not, but this is from the heart. This is my mother. This is a-- I had found it in my aunt's house, and I was really crying that day, because this is the only one picture that I have from my whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how old your mother is in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't. I have no idea at all. This picture, I remember the man from the left hand side was Liam Bruno. He must have been in his 30s, and we called him father, because he was much older than we were. Then it's myself, and next to me, it's Abe, my husband. And the other one is [INAUDIBLE]. I don't even remember his last name at all. But this was one of the very beginning that we were taking pictures, because that skirt was made from a sheet. And my blouse also was made from a different color sheet. We had no clothes yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was right after Buchenwald, or maybe even in Buchenwald. I am not sure anymore. But I think it was after Buchenwald. This picture was taken in Landsberg, not much before we were getting married. But my husband is still wearing his shirt that I was making for him in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your husband on the left and you on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right, but we weren't married then yet. This was just before. These are people from my wedding. On the left hand side, I don't remember anymore the names so good, but they were very close friends for us. We lived in the same block. And to the right hand side, and all the way on the end of the right hand side, is our friend, Benny, who is still our friend. We keeping up with him. He just came to my 50th wedding anniversary, and we're quite close. And one of them is in now Israel, that, you know, they moved to Israel, and we lost in touch. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of your wedding. And where was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wedding was in Landsberg. And we had about 100 people at the wedding that we made ourselves. I made my own wedding gown, and my own flowers, and everything. And after that, my poor wedding gown must have gone about-- I don't know, a couple hundred people wear it, because we couldn't get no material. And I don't even know how I got to it, but I just managed to get it and made a gown. Because most of the people who got married, they just had a veil on. They didn't even have wedding gowns. But I was kind of looking for the invitation, which I have. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this wedding taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a guest house. It was a very nice place. And as you see from the left hand side, there is a musician there. We had everything, but unfortunately, the best thing, we didn't have. We didn't have none of our parents, or anybody, to really enjoy it with us. OK, this is my invitation from my wedding. It says, 'An invitation' on the top. And then it says the place where it's going to come up. And it was in a guest house. And my name and my husband's name, and the date. And also, I want to tell you that we couldn't get no food those days, so people were helping us to get the chickens from the farm, and exchange for the cigarettes, because that's the only way we could buy something, and for coffee that we weren't using up. When we were working, we were getting paid by coffee and cigarettes, and food like that. And they took it to the German farms, and we were getting the goose and turkeys and whatever. And the girls were helping to clean, and everything. This is the picture, what you see, on the sewing machine. I was making shirts for the boys in Israeli Army. That was in 1948. We had a Sachnut in Landsberg, and I was volunteering to work in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the shirt factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the shirt factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the second.

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well-- just--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, nope, we're--

speaker: interviewee
text: --this is me. How could I explain that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You're the woman on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember who that is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just another girl. We had many girls, you know, taking turns working on it, and that's her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'm going to start to the left with my only granddaughter. And then I have one of my little grandsons, who's Elli. And then I have my son-in-law, who is Norman, and my daughter after him, it's Barbara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she in the pink?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the pink. And my oldest grandson, it's in black, in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Jonathan. Then my little grandson is Joshy. And right behind him, it's Alex.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are-- this is your daughter's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my daughter's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken like a few months back. It's not a very old picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture is from the summer of 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. OK. This is-- to the left is my son. And now, he's planning to marry this little girl-- a lady, actually-- who, unfortunately, lost her husband. And then she has a little daughter that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Debbie. Debbie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph, Joe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken about three months ago, so it was in 1966.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, '96. I'm getting confused here. This is Megan. That will be my son's future little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is Megan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years old. She's nine years old.
